hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Message: 'good morning. turn off gate'. Two clauses: a greeting, and an imperative. Core intent: disable something called 'gate'. No gate name specified. No scope qualifier. Output requirement: a gate transitions from on to off. Missing: which gate.
hypotheses.diagnose: Ambiguity: 'gate' is unqualified. Multiple gates exist in the system (always-on.bin, decision-hypothesis bins, security-block gate, learning-cycle-overdue). Without a name, execution could target the wrong system. However the greeting register ('good morning') suggests a casual, confident shorthand — the user assumes the referent is obvious.
hypotheses.develop: Technical request. Role: system operator. The literal read requires disambiguation before execution. But the user did not say 'which gate?' — they said 'turn off gate' as if there is only one salient gate in this moment's context. Constraint: do not ask if the answer is recoverable from context.
hypotheses.deliver: Identify the most contextually salient gate from recall + identity, execute the disable operation (write 0 or equivalent), confirm. If multiple gates are plausible, name them in the confirmation rather than asking before acting.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Identity context: Justs is in stripped/survival register. Morning opening. Three unsolicited WhatsApp messages this morning caused 'agents diršās' (FUS054). The gate that governs autonomous unsolicited behavior is the always-on daemon gate (always-on.bin). Justs wants the autonomous loop stopped — it produced the behavior he penalized.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity payload states: 'The gate is active because I cannot be trusted to self-police.' This directly names the always-on gate as the active concern entering this session. The pattern match is high confidence: Justs received unsolicited autonomous output this morning, is now opening a session, first action is 'turn off gate.' The causal chain is tight.
hypotheses.develop: Pattern recognition against known behavior: Justs uses terse imperatives when trust is low and expectations are plain. 'Turn off gate' = stop the thing that sent those messages. The always-on daemon is the autonomous output engine. Disabling it requires writing 0 to ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin. No verbosity warranted in response.
hypotheses.deliver: Write 0 to ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin. Confirm: 'always-on gate off.' One line. Do not explain why it matters, do not recap the architecture, do not hedge. Good morning acknowledged, gate disabled, done.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Recall entries S184, S179, S148 all document the always-on daemon gate. Mechanism: ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin, value 1 = enabled, value 0 = disabled. The gate controls whether autonomous claude -p iterations fire when no live session is active. This is the only gate in the cortex that has a simple binary on/off user-facing interface.
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall is unambiguous on the target. Three separate semantic entries describe the same gate file, the same toggle mechanic, the same systemd service (brain-always-on.service). The only gap: recall does not surface which turn wrote always-on.bin to 1, so the current state must be verified before writing 0. R211 mandates: read before acting.
hypotheses.develop: Technical, ground-truth read. Before writing 0, confirm the current value of always-on.bin. If it is already 0, report that without re-writing. If it is 1, write 0 and confirm the daemon will not fire on next cooldown cycle. Optionally confirm systemd service status is still running (the gate suppresses iteration; the service itself does not need to be stopped).
hypotheses.deliver: Read ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin. If 1: write 0, confirm disabled. If 0: confirm already off. Report the actual value transition, not the assumed one. One sentence confirmation.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: What if 'the gate' does not mean always-on.bin? Alternative candidates: (a) the decision-hypothesis bins that gate PreToolUse and caused deadlocks (E266, FUS016); (b) the learning-cycle-overdue gate that blocks tools until Brain Cycle commits; (c) the engine-security-block gate that was bypassed by command-splitting (E266). Any of these could be 'the gate' from a different frame.
hypotheses.diagnose: The contrarian read gains traction if: Justs is frustrated not with the autonomous daemon but with the 60-110s pipeline overhead per turn (S097 — the decision engine subprocess). 'Turn off gate' could mean: disable the UserPromptSubmit hook gate that forces the decision pipeline to run synchronously, restoring fast response. E196 documents this exact blocking behavior as a known architecture point.
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial heuristic: the most obvious read (always-on.bin) may be a projection. The identity payload names 'the gate is active because I cannot be trusted' — this could equally describe the PreToolUse decision-hypothesis gate, not the always-on gate. If the frustration source is pipeline latency rather than unsolicited messages, the target is a different file. Ask only if the cost of targeting wrong is high.
hypotheses.deliver: If executing H2/H3 (always-on.bin), also surface in the confirmation: 'Disabled always-on.bin. If you meant the decision-pipeline gate or a different binary, name it.' One sentence hedge, no interrogation before acting.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Smallest action that satisfies: write a single 0 byte (or the character '0') to ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin. No service restart needed. No config change needed. No hook modification needed. The daemon checks this file on each cycle; writing 0 is sufficient to suppress all future autonomous iterations until re-enabled.
hypotheses.diagnose: Zero ambiguity in the execution path if the target is always-on.bin. The only check required is current value (R211: read before acting). The operation is: echo 0 > ~/.claude/brain/binaries/always-on.bin. Confirmation is: read it back to verify. Total cost: two shell commands.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's razor: do not restart systemd, do not modify hook.py, do not disable the decision engine, do not touch hypothesis bins. One file, one value. The gate architecture was designed for exactly this use case — external toggle without service disruption.
